data.title: BYD (SEHK:1211) Valuation Check As P/E Premium Contrasts With Cash Flow Upside Signals
data.description:
Advertisement

Recent share performance and business scale

Without a clear single news catalyst, you might be looking at BYD (SEHK:1211) simply because its recent share performance and business size stand out among Hong Kong listed auto and battery groups.

Over the past month, BYD shares have returned about 5.9%, while the past 3 months show a modest 1.3% decline. On a 1 year view, the total return figure provided is 14.2%.

The company reports revenue of CN¥841.1b and net income of CN¥38.3b. This reflects a large-scale operation across automobiles, batteries and related services. Annual revenue growth is given as 10.8%, with net income growth at 16.3%.

At the last close of HK$102.80, some valuation metrics in the data suggest a potential gap between market price and certain fair value estimates. However, these approaches depend heavily on the assumptions behind each model.

See our latest analysis for BYD.

Recent trading points to rebuilding momentum, with a 1 day share price return of 4.6% and a 7 day share price return of 3.8%, set against a 1 year total shareholder return of 14.2% that reflects steadier long term gains.

If BYD has you thinking more broadly about the auto space, it could be a good time to see what else is on the move among auto manufacturers.

So with BYD trading at HK$102.80 alongside fair value and price target estimates that sit higher, is the current share price a genuine gap you can exploit, or is the market already factoring in future growth?

Price to earnings of 21.7x: Is it justified?

On the latest numbers, BYD trades on a P/E of 21.7x, while our fair value work points to a lower P/E of 13.8x and the shares sit below some cash flow based estimates such as the SWS DCF model fair value of HK$136 versus the last close at HK$102.80.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price with earnings per share and is a common way for investors to judge how much they are paying for each unit of profit. For a large auto and battery group like BYD, a higher P/E can reflect expectations for further earnings progress as well as the breadth of its businesses across vehicles, batteries and related services.

Here, the 21.7x P/E sits above both the Asian auto industry average of 18.2x and a peer average of 8.3x. This suggests the market is assigning BYD a richer earnings tag than many regional and peer names. Compared with the estimated fair P/E of 13.8x, the gap is also wide. This highlights a level that the market could move towards if sentiment or earnings expectations change.

Explore the SWS fair ratio for BYD

Result: Price to earnings of 21.7x (OVERVALUED)

However, you still need to watch for earnings expectations resetting if sentiment toward higher P/E auto names cools or if analyst price targets are revised lower.

Find out about the key risks to this BYD narrative.

Another view: Cash flow says something different

If you put the earnings multiple to one side and look at our SWS DCF model, you get a different message. On this view, BYD at HK$102.80 sits about 24.4% below an estimated fair value of HK$136, which points to potential upside rather than stretch.

That gap matters because it shows how sensitive conclusions can be to the tool you use, and to the assumptions built into it. If profits or cash flows play out differently, could this apparent cushion shrink or widen from here?

data.title: Northwest Bancshares (NWBI) Net Interest Margin Of 3.69% Reinforces Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Northwest Bancshares (NWBI) has put up a solid finish to FY 2025, with Q4 total revenue of US$172.2 million and basic EPS of US$0.31. The company also reported trailing 12 month revenue of US$599.1 million and EPS of US$0.93, alongside earnings growth of 25.8% over the last year. Over recent periods the company has seen total revenue move from US$498.1 million on a trailing basis in Q4 2024 to US$599.1 million in Q4 2025, while trailing EPS has gone from US$0.79 to US$0.93 as net margin held around 21%. This sets up a results season where profitability is front and center for investors.

See our full analysis for Northwest Bancshares.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely followed narratives around growth, risk, and income potential for Northwest Bancshares.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Loan book edges past US$13b with margin near 3.7%

  • Total loans reached about US$13.0b in Q4 2025, with a net interest margin of 3.69% and a cost to income ratio of 59.52%.
  • What stands out for a more optimistic view is that loan balances moved from roughly US$11.2b in Q1 2025 to about US$13.0b by Q4, while the trailing 12 month net profit margin sat at 21%. This gives bulls a story of a larger loan book still earning solid spreads.
    • Supporters pointing to earnings momentum can lean on trailing EPS of US$0.93 and net income of US$126.0m over the last year, alongside the 25.8% earnings growth figure that has already been reported.
    • At the same time, Q4 2025 net interest margin of 3.69% compares with 3.33% in Q3 2024, which fits a bullish argument that core banking profitability metrics have held up while scale has increased.
To see how that loan growth and margin profile fit into the bigger bullish and cautious stories around Northwest Bancshares, check out the latest narrative views on the company. 📊 Read the full Northwest Bancshares Consensus Narrative.

Non performing loans rise to about US$108m

  • Non performing loans moved from US$59.4m in Q1 2025 to US$107.9m in Q4 2025, after being US$62.1m in Q4 2024.
  • Critics focus on the more cautious angle that credit quality is a watch point, and the step up in non performing loans gives that view some backing, even alongside the reported 21% net margin.
    • Bears can point to the move from US$62.1m of non performing loans in Q4 2024 to US$107.9m in Q4 2025 as a sign that more loans are not currently paying as agreed, which they see as a risk to future earnings.
    • They may also link this with the large US$42.8m one off loss recorded in the last 12 months, arguing that both items remind investors that recent strong earnings growth figures can sit alongside credit and one time loss pressures.

Valuation gap vs DCF fair value and rich P/E

  • The current share price of US$12.76 sits well below the stated DCF fair value of about US$25.30, while the stock trades on a P/E of 14.8x compared with peers at 12.9x and the US Banks industry at 11.8x.
  • What is interesting for you as an investor is that the same data that suggests a large gap to DCF fair value also shows Northwest Bancshares on a richer P/E than peers, so bullish and bearish takes both find support in the numbers.
    • Supporters often highlight that the share price is roughly 49.6% below the provided DCF fair value of US$25.30, and combine that with 25.8% trailing earnings growth and a 6.27% dividend yield to argue there is room for value and income at the same time.
    • Skeptics instead point to the 14.8x P/E relative to 12.9x for peers and 11.8x for the broader US Banks group, saying that even with the valuation gap to DCF fair value, the market is already paying more per dollar of trailing earnings than for many similar banks.

data.title: Sopharma AD's (BUL:SFA) five-year total shareholder returns outpace the underlying earnings growth
data.description:
The most you can lose on any stock (assuming you don't use leverage) is 100% of your money. But on the bright side, if you buy shares in a high quality company at the right price, you can gain well over 100%. One great example is Sopharma AD (BUL:SFA) which saw its share price drive 223% higher over five years. It's also good to see the share price up 33% over the last quarter. But this move may well have been assisted by the reasonably buoyant market (up 17% in 90 days).

While this past week has detracted from the company's five-year return, let's look at the recent trends of the underlying business and see if the gains have been in alignment.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

While the efficient markets hypothesis continues to be taught by some, it has been proven that markets are over-reactive dynamic systems, and investors are not always rational. One way to examine how market sentiment has changed over time is to look at the interaction between a company's share price and its earnings per share (EPS).

Over half a decade, Sopharma AD managed to grow its earnings per share at 4.1% a year. This EPS growth is slower than the share price growth of 26% per year, over the same period. So it's fair to assume the market has a higher opinion of the business than it did five years ago. And that's hardly shocking given the track record of growth.

The graphic below depicts how EPS has changed over time (unveil the exact values by clicking on the image).

This free interactive report on Sopharma AD's earnings, revenue and cash flow is a great place to start, if you want to investigate the stock further.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. Arguably, the TSR gives a more comprehensive picture of the return generated by a stock. In the case of Sopharma AD, it has a TSR of 339% for the last 5 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. This is largely a result of its dividend payments!

A Different Perspective

It's nice to see that Sopharma AD shareholders have received a total shareholder return of 99% over the last year. And that does include the dividend. Since the one-year TSR is better than the five-year TSR (the latter coming in at 34% per year), it would seem that the stock's performance has improved in recent times. Someone with an optimistic perspective could view the recent improvement in TSR as indicating that the business itself is getting better with time. I find it very interesting to look at share price over the long term as a proxy for business performance. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. For example, we've discovered 3 warning signs for Sopharma AD (2 are a bit unpleasant!) that you should be aware of before investing here.

If you would prefer to check out another company -- one with potentially superior financials -- then do not miss this free list of companies that have proven they can grow earnings.

data.title: Mennica Polska (WSE:MNC) Seems To Use Debt Quite Sensibly
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. As with many other companies Mennica Polska S.A. (WSE:MNC) makes use of debt. But should shareholders be worried about its use of debt?

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Generally speaking, debt only becomes a real problem when a company can't easily pay it off, either by raising capital or with its own cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. While that is not too common, we often do see indebted companies permanently diluting shareholders because lenders force them to raise capital at a distressed price. Of course, plenty of companies use debt to fund growth, without any negative consequences. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

What Is Mennica Polska's Net Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 Mennica Polska had debt of zł277.9m, up from zł306.0k in one year. However, because it has a cash reserve of zł106.9m, its net debt is less, at about zł171.1m.

How Strong Is Mennica Polska's Balance Sheet?

We can see from the most recent balance sheet that Mennica Polska had liabilities of zł1.08b falling due within a year, and liabilities of zł196.3m due beyond that. Offsetting this, it had zł106.9m in cash and zł139.6m in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by zł1.03b.

This deficit isn't so bad because Mennica Polska is worth zł2.57b, and thus could probably raise enough capital to shore up its balance sheet, if the need arose. But we definitely want to keep our eyes open to indications that its debt is bringing too much risk.

See our latest analysis for Mennica Polska

In order to size up a company's debt relative to its earnings, we calculate its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) divided by its interest expense (its interest cover). This way, we consider both the absolute quantum of the debt, as well as the interest rates paid on it.

We'd say that Mennica Polska's moderate net debt to EBITDA ratio ( being 1.5), indicates prudence when it comes to debt. And its commanding EBIT of 1k times its interest expense, implies the debt load is as light as a peacock feather. It is just as well that Mennica Polska's load is not too heavy, because its EBIT was down 44% over the last year. When a company sees its earnings tank, it can sometimes find its relationships with its lenders turn sour. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But you can't view debt in total isolation; since Mennica Polska will need earnings to service that debt. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

Finally, a company can only pay off debt with cold hard cash, not accounting profits. So we always check how much of that EBIT is translated into free cash flow. Over the last three years, Mennica Polska actually produced more free cash flow than EBIT. That sort of strong cash generation warms our hearts like a puppy in a bumblebee suit.

Our View

Mennica Polska's EBIT growth rate was a real negative on this analysis, although the other factors we considered were considerably better. In particular, we are dazzled with its interest cover. When we consider all the factors mentioned above, we do feel a bit cautious about Mennica Polska's use of debt. While debt does have its upside in higher potential returns, we think shareholders should definitely consider how debt levels might make the stock more risky. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. However, not all investment risk resides within the balance sheet - far from it. To that end, you should be aware of the 1 warning sign we've spotted with Mennica Polska .

At the end of the day, it's often better to focus on companies that are free from net debt. You can access our special list of such companies (all with a track record of profit growth). It's free.

data.title: Is Vinci (ENXTPA:DG) Fairly Priced After Strong Multi Year Share Price Gains
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Vinci shares still offer fair value at around €118.75, you are not alone. This article is designed to help you make sense of that question using clear valuation tools.
  • The stock has returned 3.5% over the last 7 days, sits at a 0.8% decline over 30 days, is 2.0% lower year to date, yet is up 18.3% over 1 year, 29.0% over 3 years and 77.8% over 5 years. This gives you a mixed picture to interpret.
  • Recent price moves are set against ongoing interest in Vinci as a major European infrastructure and concessions group, with investors watching how its project pipeline and capital allocation decisions influence long term prospects. Broader sector attention on large construction and infrastructure names has also kept Vinci on the radar for investors comparing risks and potential returns within capital goods.
  • On our checks, Vinci records a valuation score of 3 out of 6, which suggests the stock screens as undervalued on half of the measures we use. Next we will walk through those approaches before finishing with a more complete way to think about what fair value could mean for you.

Find out why Vinci's 18.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Vinci Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a single present value. Here we are using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that focuses on the cash Vinci can generate for shareholders.

Vinci’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about €7.17b. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates these further out. In this model, Vinci’s free cash flow is projected at around €4.98b in 2029, with a sequence of annual projections between 2026 and 2035 used to build the valuation in two stages.

Adding up all those discounted cash flows gives an estimated intrinsic value of €105.97 per share. Against a current share price of roughly €118.75, the DCF output implies the shares are about 12.1% above this estimate, which indicates Vinci screens as overvalued on this model alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Vinci may be overvalued by 12.1%. Discover 872 undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Vinci.

Approach 2: Vinci Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Vinci, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how much the market is willing to pay today for each €1 of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower multiple.

Vinci currently trades on a P/E of 13.84x. That sits below both the Construction industry average of 15.35x and the peer average of 23.82x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Vinci is 24.49x, which is a proprietary view of what Vinci’s P/E might look like after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and specific risks.

The Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison, because it adjusts for Vinci’s own characteristics rather than assuming all Construction stocks should trade on the same multiple.

With a Fair Ratio of 24.49x compared to the current 13.84x, Vinci screens as undervalued on this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover 1424 companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Approach 1: Artisan Partners Asset Management Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model asks a simple question: are shareholders earning more on the company’s equity than they are requiring as a return, and how long might that gap persist? For Artisan Partners Asset Management, the model uses an average return on equity of 53.69% and a stable book value of $4.73 per share, both based on median figures from the past 5 years.

On this foundation, the model estimates stable earnings of $2.54 per share, a cost of equity of $0.39 per share, and an excess return of $2.15 per share. Put plainly, the company is modeled as generating earnings above the return that equity holders are assumed to require on its capital base.

When these excess returns are projected forward and discounted, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $47.84 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $43.81, this implies the stock is around 8.4% undervalued, which is a relatively small gap that points to pricing that is close to the model’s estimate of fair value.

data.title: Return Trends At Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia (BVMF:EQMA3B) Aren't Appealing
data.description:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. So, when we ran our eye over Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's (BVMF:EQMA3B) trend of ROCE, we liked what we saw.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.13 = R$1.2b ÷ (R$12b - R$2.3b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has an ROCE of 13%. That's a relatively normal return on capital, and it's around the 12% generated by the Electric Utilities industry.

Check out our latest analysis for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia

Historical performance is a great place to start when researching a stock so above you can see the gauge for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's ROCE against it's prior returns. If you're interested in investigating Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's past further, check out this free graph covering Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's past earnings, revenue and cash flow.

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

While the returns on capital are good, they haven't moved much. The company has employed 59% more capital in the last five years, and the returns on that capital have remained stable at 13%. 13% is a pretty standard return, and it provides some comfort knowing that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has consistently earned this amount. Stable returns in this ballpark can be unexciting, but if they can be maintained over the long run, they often provide nice rewards to shareholders.

The Key Takeaway

The main thing to remember is that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has proven its ability to continually reinvest at respectable rates of return. However, over the last five years, the stock has only delivered a 24% return to shareholders who held over that period. That's why it could be worth your time looking into this stock further to discover if it has more traits of a multi-bagger.

data.title: We Think Empreendimentos Pague Menos (BVMF:PGMN3) Is Taking Some Risk With Its Debt
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. We can see that Empreendimentos Pague Menos S.A. (BVMF:PGMN3) does use debt in its business. But the more important question is: how much risk is that debt creating?

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more frequent (but still costly) occurrence is where a company must issue shares at bargain-basement prices, permanently diluting shareholders, just to shore up its balance sheet. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

How Much Debt Does Empreendimentos Pague Menos Carry?

As you can see below, at the end of September 2025, Empreendimentos Pague Menos had R$1.75b of debt, up from R$1.46b a year ago. Click the image for more detail. However, it also had R$106.3m in cash, and so its net debt is R$1.64b.

How Strong Is Empreendimentos Pague Menos' Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Empreendimentos Pague Menos had liabilities of R$3.28b due within a year, and liabilities of R$3.13b falling due after that. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of R$106.3m as well as receivables valued at R$1.45b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by R$4.85b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of R$4.25b, we think shareholders really should watch Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

See our latest analysis for Empreendimentos Pague Menos

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). This way, we consider both the absolute quantum of the debt, as well as the interest rates paid on it.

While Empreendimentos Pague Menos has a quite reasonable net debt to EBITDA multiple of 2.4, its interest cover seems weak, at 1.7. This does suggest the company is paying fairly high interest rates. In any case, it's safe to say the company has meaningful debt. Importantly, Empreendimentos Pague Menos grew its EBIT by 37% over the last twelve months, and that growth will make it easier to handle its debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But it is future earnings, more than anything, that will determine Empreendimentos Pague Menos's ability to maintain a healthy balance sheet going forward. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So we clearly need to look at whether that EBIT is leading to corresponding free cash flow. Over the last three years, Empreendimentos Pague Menos reported free cash flow worth 19% of its EBIT, which is really quite low. For us, cash conversion that low sparks a little paranoia about is ability to extinguish debt.

Our View

On the face of it, Empreendimentos Pague Menos's level of total liabilities left us tentative about the stock, and its interest cover was no more enticing than the one empty restaurant on the busiest night of the year. But on the bright side, its EBIT growth rate is a good sign, and makes us more optimistic. Once we consider all the factors above, together, it seems to us that Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt is making it a bit risky. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but we'd generally feel more comfortable with less leverage. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. We've identified 4 warning signs with Empreendimentos Pague Menos (at least 2 which are a bit concerning) , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

At the end of the day, it's often better to focus on companies that are free from net debt. You can access our special list of such companies (all with a track record of profit growth). It's free.

data.title: The past year for Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles (NSE:TMPV) investors has not been profitable
data.description:
Investors can approximate the average market return by buying an index fund. While individual stocks can be big winners, plenty more fail to generate satisfactory returns. That downside risk was realized by Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles Limited (NSE:TMPV) shareholders over the last year, as the share price declined 53%. That's disappointing when you consider the market returned 7.9%. Longer term shareholders haven't suffered as badly, since the stock is down a comparatively less painful 25% in three years. The falls have accelerated recently, with the share price down 17% in the last three months.

With that in mind, it's worth seeing if the company's underlying fundamentals have been the driver of long term performance, or if there are some discrepancies.

To paraphrase Benjamin Graham: Over the short term the market is a voting machine, but over the long term it's a weighing machine. By comparing earnings per share (EPS) and share price changes over time, we can get a feel for how investor attitudes to a company have morphed over time.

Unfortunately Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles reported an EPS drop of 69% for the last year. The share price fall of 53% isn't as bad as the reduction in earnings per share. So the market may not be too worried about the EPS figure, at the moment -- or it may have expected earnings to drop faster.

The graphic below depicts how EPS has changed over time (unveil the exact values by clicking on the image).

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. Whereas the share price return only reflects the change in the share price, the TSR includes the value of dividends (assuming they were reinvested) and the benefit of any discounted capital raising or spin-off. It's fair to say that the TSR gives a more complete picture for stocks that pay a dividend. In the case of Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles, it has a TSR of -4.3% for the last 1 year. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. The dividends paid by the company have thusly boosted the total shareholder return.

A Different Perspective

Investors in Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles had a tough year, with a total loss of 4.3% (including dividends), against a market gain of about 7.9%. However, keep in mind that even the best stocks will sometimes underperform the market over a twelve month period. Longer term investors wouldn't be so upset, since they would have made 17%, each year, over five years. If the fundamental data continues to indicate long term sustainable growth, the current sell-off could be an opportunity worth considering. I find it very interesting to look at share price over the long term as a proxy for business performance. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. data.title: Strong week for PBG (BVMF:PTBL3) shareholders doesn't alleviate pain of five-year loss
data.description:
PBG S.A. (BVMF:PTBL3) shareholders should be happy to see the share price up 15% in the last month. But don't envy holders -- looking back over 5 years the returns have been really bad. The share price has failed to impress anyone , down a sizable 62% during that time. So we're not so sure if the recent bounce should be celebrated. However, in the best case scenario (far from fait accompli), this improved performance might be sustained.

The recent uptick of 14% could be a positive sign of things to come, so let's take a look at historical fundamentals.

PBG wasn't profitable in the last twelve months, it is unlikely we'll see a strong correlation between its share price and its earnings per share (EPS). Arguably revenue is our next best option. Shareholders of unprofitable companies usually desire strong revenue growth. That's because it's hard to be confident a company will be sustainable if revenue growth is negligible, and it never makes a profit.

In the last half decade, PBG saw its revenue increase by 11% per year. That's a fairly respectable growth rate. The share price, meanwhile, has fallen 10% compounded, over five years. It seems probably that the business has failed to live up to initial expectations. A pessimistic market can create opportunities.

What About The Total Shareholder Return (TSR)?

Investors should note that there's a difference between PBG's total shareholder return (TSR) and its share price change, which we've covered above. Arguably the TSR is a more complete return calculation because it accounts for the value of dividends (as if they were reinvested), along with the hypothetical value of any discounted capital that have been offered to shareholders. Its history of dividend payouts mean that PBG's TSR, which was a 55% drop over the last 5 years, was not as bad as the share price return.

A Different Perspective

data.title: Padtec Holding S.A. (BVMF:PDTC3) Held Back By Insufficient Growth Even After Shares Climb 32%
data.description:
Padtec Holding S.A. (BVMF:PDTC3) shares have continued their recent momentum with a 32% gain in the last month alone. Looking back a bit further, it's encouraging to see the stock is up 37% in the last year.

Even after such a large jump in price, Padtec Holding's price-to-sales (or "P/S") ratio of 0.4x might still make it look like a buy right now compared to the Communications industry in Brazil, where around half of the companies have P/S ratios above 2x and even P/S above 7x are quite common. Although, it's not wise to just take the P/S at face value as there may be an explanation why it's limited.

How Has Padtec Holding Performed Recently?

Padtec Holding has been doing a good job lately as it's been growing revenue at a solid pace. It might be that many expect the respectable revenue performance to degrade substantially, which has repressed the P/S. Those who are bullish on Padtec Holding will be hoping that this isn't the case, so that they can pick up the stock at a lower valuation.

Although there are no analyst estimates available for Padtec Holding, take a look at this free data-rich visualisation to see how the company stacks up on earnings, revenue and cash flow.

What Are Revenue Growth Metrics Telling Us About The Low P/S?

In order to justify its P/S ratio, Padtec Holding would need to produce sluggish growth that's trailing the industry.

Retrospectively, the last year delivered a decent 8.9% gain to the company's revenues. Still, lamentably revenue has fallen 11% in aggregate from three years ago, which is disappointing. So unfortunately, we have to acknowledge that the company has not done a great job of growing revenue over that time.

Weighing that medium-term revenue trajectory against the broader industry's one-year forecast for expansion of 27% shows it's an unpleasant look.

With this in mind, we understand why Padtec Holding's P/S is lower than most of its industry peers. Nonetheless, there's no guarantee the P/S has reached a floor yet with revenue going in reverse. There's potential for the P/S to fall to even lower levels if the company doesn't improve its top-line growth.

What We Can Learn From Padtec Holding's P/S?

Despite Padtec Holding's share price climbing recently, its P/S still lags most other companies. While the price-to-sales ratio shouldn't be the defining factor in whether you buy a stock or not, it's quite a capable barometer of revenue expectations.

It's no surprise that Padtec Holding maintains its low P/S off the back of its sliding revenue over the medium-term. At this stage investors feel the potential for an improvement in revenue isn't great enough to justify a higher P/S ratio. Given the current circumstances, it seems unlikely that the share price will experience any significant movement in either direction in the near future if recent medium-term revenue trends persist.
